steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The goal is a "historical overview of the Shackleton expedition" — meaning the expedition's own events in sequence, not books, films, or discoveries that came afterward. The key notes for the expedition itself are 1914 (departure), 1915 (ship sunk), and 1916 (crew rescued).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Alfred Lansing wrote about the history of Shackletonʼs 1914–16 expedition in the book *Endurance: Shackletonʼs Incredible Voyage* (1959); years later, in 2001, George Butler released a documentary about the expedition.: This describes post-expedition documentation (a 1959 book and 2001 film), not the expedition's own events. ✗
  • Choice B — In 1914, the Shackleton expedition sailed to Antarctica, where, in 1916, they rescued the crew of a ship that had sunk, *Endurance* (the wreckage of which was discovered in 2022).: This distorts the facts — it was Shackleton's own crew that was rescued in 1916, not a separate crew. ✗
  • Choice C — Leaving in 1914 for Antarctica, Shackleton and his crew underwent many harrowing sea-ice adventures, including losing their ship in 1915, before being rescued in 1916.: This covers all three core expedition events in chronological order — departure, ship lost, crew rescued — providing a true historical overview. ✓
  • Choice D — Shackletonʼs expedition has inspired a 1959 book, a 2001 film, and a 2022 discovery.: This lists only post-expedition cultural and scientific legacy, not the expedition's own events. ✗
That matches Choice C — Leaving in 1914 for Antarctica, Shackleton and his crew underwent many harrowing sea-ice adventures, including losing their ship in 1915, before being rescued in 1916..
